The high nutrient content in traditional foods and vegetables means they can help alleviate malnutrition. In Kenya, for instance, stunting in children under five years in 2008-2012 was at 35.3%, going down to 26% by 2014.
Additionally, many developing countries, including Kenya, are battling a new problem - the rise in non-communicable diseases like cancer and heart diseases. Traditional vegetables show high antioxidant activity and can be useful for preventing these diseases.
When a species loses its value in a community or society, it is likely to disappear. When the species is lost, it takes with it all its associated intangible cultural heritage. Promoting indigenous foods promotes conservation of species (and biodiversity), which is good for the planet. It also slows or halts cultural erosion.
Patrick Maundu, Ethnobotanist, National Museums of Kenya

Original_Search_Title: Nasida Attracts Over $500m Investment to Nasarawa, Says Md
Headline: Nigeria: Nasida Attracts Over $500m Investment to Nasarawa, Says Md
Body_Text:
The Nasarawa State Investment Development Agency (NASIDA) has said it has attracted over $500 million investment, both domestic and foreign, into key sectors of Nasarawa State.
The Managing Director of NASIDA, Ibrahim Abdullahi who disclosed this at a news briefing in Lafia added that the feat was achieved over the past three years of the coming to office of Governor Abdullahi Sule.
He added that, during the period, the state had closed significantly at 13 Public Private Partnership (PPP) and as well implemented eight business environment initiatives.
He said: "We have welcome a pictorial of investors and investments in the state. And notably Flour Mills of Nigeria has signed an agreement to acquire and do a development of 20,000 hectres sugarcane plantation and sugar industrial estate in Nasarawa around Toto. That has an investment initially at a minimum of $300 million.
"We also have a pipeline of different investors coming to invest generally in agriculture sector from rice production to sugar production and cassava production. We have signed a PPP agreement for the Nasarawa State Transport Company and the consension of the bus terminus. Thereafter, in the second phase of the Auta Balefi Recycling Plant project, we hoped to create a waste to energy project. That is a project that has attracted about $33 million in funding, both from domestic and foreign investment.
"We also signed the Peninsula Joint Venture agreement with the private sector which is government's efforts to build sustainable housing clusters in the corridors of Abuja," he explained. NASIDA has signed an agreement to develop the Nasarawa Technology Village to help build and position the state as an emerging centre for digital economy."

Original_Search_Title: Cost of Sales, FX Loss Weakens Nigerian Breweries Profit
Headline: Nigeria: Cost of Sales, FX Loss Weakens Nigerian Breweries Profit
Body_Text:
While Nigerian Breweries Plc grew its profit and dividend for shareholders in audited result and accounts for period ended December 31, 2021, the company was faced with hike in costs of sales and net loss on foreign exchange transactions during the period under review.
The deprecation of Naira and double-digit inflation rate played a critical role in Nigerian Breweries 2021 audited result and accounts, coupled with tax expenses that nearly tripled that of 2020 results.
In May of 2021, the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) devalued the naira from N379 to N411.00 per dollar after adopting the Investors and Exporters (I&E) window rate and this contributed to Nigerian Breweries Plc's performance.
In the year under review, Nigerian Breweries reported about 27 per cent increase in cost of sales to N276.87billion as against N218.36billion reported in prior year results, while net loss on foreign exchange transactions grew significantly by 47.6per cent to N7.04billion in 2021 from N4.77billion reported in 2020 financial year.
The group's overall operating expenses that include cost of sales, marketing & disturbing expenses, and administrative expenses rose by nearly 30 per cent to N400.42billion in 2021 from N308.02billion reported in 2020.
Most of the key drives of Nigerian Breweries overall operating expenses are hike in raw materials and consumables, advertising & sales promotion, employee benefits and repairs & maintenance.
Specifically, Nigerian Breweries recorded 38 per cent increase cost of raw materials and consumables to N197.8billion in 2021 from N143.39billion reported in 2020, while advertising & sales promotion grew significantly by 59 per cent to N39.5billion in 2021 from N24.9billion in 2020.
In addition, employee benefits grew by 21 per cent to N49.61billion in 2021 from N40.98billion in 2020.
Recently, however, Nigeria's manufacturing sector has been experiencing great operational difficulties. This is the direct consequence of the fall oil global oil prices due to COVID-19, resulting in the devaluation of Naira and increased difficulties for manufacturing companies to source the foreign exchange they require to import raw materials required for operations.
Nigerian Breweries had announced that it achieved 57 per cent of its 60 per cent 2020 target for local sourcing of raw material for production. The company uses maize, rice as brewing adjuncts (sources of starch) in addition sorghum and cassava in the production of its products.
Meanwhile, the top-line performance, revenue for the group rose by nearly 30 per cent to N437.3billion in 2021 from n337.05billion in 2020, mostly driven by revenue generated in Nigeria.
In the 2021 audited result and accounts, the Group recorded N437.16billion sales in Nigeria from N336.9illion recorded in 2020, while sales from export dropped by 11per cent to N128.12million in 2021 from N143.9million in 2020.
Remarkably, Imported bought in materials and services that contributed to revenue grew by 84 per cent to N71.84billion in 2021 from N39.05billion in 2020, while locally sourced bought in materials and services closed 2021 at N240.74billion, an increase of 19 per cent from N201.72billion reported in 2020.
Operating Expenses/Revenue
Consequently, the over operating expenses/revenue grew from 91.38per cent in 2020 to 91.57 per cent in 2021.
Nigerian Breweries has a rich portfolio quality brands: Star larger that was launched in 1949, followed by Gulder lager beer in 1970. Maltina, the nourishing malt drink that was introduced in 1976, followed by Legend Extra Stout in 1992 and another malt drink, Amstel Malta in 1994. Henieken lager beer was re-launched into the Nigerian market in 1998. Fayrouz, the premium non-alcoholic soft drink, was launched in 2006, while Climax herbal energy drink was launched in 2010.
Following the acquisition of Sona Systems and Life Breweries in 2011, Goldberg lager, Malta Gold malt drink and Life Continental lager, were added to the brand portfolio. In 2014, it merged with Consolidate Breweries, producer of "33" Export lager beer, Willams dark ale, Turbo King dar ale. More lager beer and a malt drink, Hi Malt became part of the company's product.
In 2020, the company further expanded its portfolio of brands with the launch of two variants of the Maltina brand, Maltina pineapple and Maltina Vanilla. Other brands launched in 2020 were Amstel Malta Ultra, an extension of the Amstel Malta brand, Star Ralder Red Fruits, a variant of the Star Radler brand and Desperados, another international premium beer brand with a distinctive tequila flavor.
From the company's loss and profit figures, gross profit grew by 35 per cent to N160.4billion in 2021 from N118.69billion in 2020 to position gross margin at 36.68per cent in 2021 from 35.22per cent in 2020.
The company was able to drive its other income that grew significantly by 459 per cent to N4.63billion in 2021 from N828.1million in 2020. The increase in other income was primarily driven by sales of scrap that moved from N729.44million in 2020 to N1.45billion in 2021, and gained N2.95billion from gain on right-of-use de-recognition in 2021.
Other operating expenses revealed that Nigerian Breweries in the year under review reported about 38 per cent increase in marketing and distribution expenses to N97.3billion in 2021 from N70.7billion reported in 2020, while administrative expenses grew by 37 per cent to N26.24billion in 2021 from N19.21billion reported in 2020.
As a follow-up, profit from operating activities closed 2021 at N41.49billion from N29.61billion in 2020. While interest income on bank's deposits grew by 27.3per cent to N313.5million in 2021 from N246.28million in 2020, finance cost that included interest expenses on loans borrowings, among others dropped by 18per cent to N11.07billion in 2021 from N13.5billion in 2020.
The breakdown of in finance cost revealed that interest expenses on loans and borrowings moved to N1.12 billion in 2021 from N5.55billion in 2020, while interest expenses on overdraft gained 102per cent to N3.58billion in 2021 from N1.78billion in 2020. In addition, unwinding of discount on employee benefits dropped to N873million in 2021 from N2.01billion in 2020. In all, net finance costs dropped to N17.79billion in 2021, a decline of 1.31per cent from N18.03billion reported in 2020.
Profit & Dividend Payout
Amid operational challenges faced by Nigerian Breweries in 2021, its profit before tax gained 105 per cent to N23.7billion in 2021 from N11.6billion in 2020.
The reported N23.7billion profit before tax is the second highest declared since 2019 when a profit before tax of N23.5 billion was reported by Nigerian Breweries.
The company reached had reported N46.6billion profit before tax in 2017 on the heels of favorable operating environment.
Tax grew significantly by 162.1 per cent to N11.03billion in 2021 from N4.21billion in 2020, as income tax using the statutory tax rate that grew by per cent to N7.21billion in 2021 and tax on Right On-Use-Act that closed 2021 at N4.38billion impacted on tax expenses.
So, profit for the year increased by 72 per cent to N12.67billion in 2021 from N7.37billion reported in 2020, driving the group Basic Earnings per share to N1.57 per share in 2021 from N0.92 per share in 2020.
The management of Nigerian Breweries proposed a final dividend of N1.20 kobo per share in 2021 as against N0.69 kobo per share base on the issued share capital of 8,075,831,900 ordinary shares of 50kobo each subject to approval by shareholders.
The management of Nigerian Breweries recommend to shareholders a total dividend of N12.9 billion that is, N1.60 kobo only per ordinary share of 50 kobo each. In 2020, the management had declared
The total dividend of 2021 financial year is comprised of an interim dividend of N3.23billion, that is, 40 kobo per share, which was declared in October 2021, and a final dividend of N9.69 billion kobo per share.
Trade & Other Receivables
Nigerian Breweries grew total assets by nearly nine per cent to N485.5billion in 2021 from N445.9billion reported in 2020, driven by growth in trade & other receivables and property, plant and equipment in non-current and current assets.
About 21 per cent increase in property, plant and equipment in non-current assets to N257.22billion in 2021 from N213.4billion impacted on total assets. The company reported 72.3 per cent increase in inventories to N62.19billion in 2021 from N36.09billion reported in 2020 to increase current assets to N120.17billion in 2021 from N93.2billion in 2020.
The group reported nearly seven per cent increase in total equity to N171.9billion in 2021 from N161.08billion in 2020. The growth in total equity was driven by 8.4 per cent increase in retained earnings from N83.09billion in 2020 to N90.09billion in 2021.
However, total liabilities that rose by 10.2 per cent to N313.61billion in 2021 from N284.7billion in 2020 was driven by 63 per cent increase in trade and other payable to N226.42billion in 2021 from N139.2billion in 2020.
From the balance sheet position, current liabilities that comprises of Trade and other payables moved to N272billion in 2021 from while non-current liabilities dropped by 45 per cent to N41.08billion in 2021 from N74.2billion recorded in 2020.
Nigerian Breweries total loans and borrowings n 2021 was N31.37 billion as against N91.45 billion reported in 2020. This simply means that the company's loans and borrowings contributed about 6.5 per cent to total assets in 2021 as against 20.5 per cent in 2020.
A Good Year
Nigerian breweries in 2021 had a wonderful year but its performance at reducing the cost of sales and net loss on foreign exchange should be a major concern.
The company continued to grow amid adverse impact of competition with other breweries companies operating in the country. All through 2019, the operating environment remained a challenging one as the year witnessed reduced consumer purchasing power and increased cost of doing business mainly due to inflation.
Notwithstanding, the company is focused on maintaining cost leadership and market Leadership supported by Innovation, which has helped to maintain strong results and deliver a good return on investment to shareholders.
The future outlook depends on the commitments and choices made by the board and management in terms of attracting more liquidity and increased business operations.

Original_Search_Title: EU Will Sustain Investment Partnerships With Nigeria - Vestager
Headline: Nigeria: EU Will Sustain Investment Partnerships With Nigeria - Vestager
Body_Text:
The European Union (EU) Commission yesterday said it would continue to partner Nigeria on business opportunities, investments and other developmental issues.
Executive Vice President of the European Union (EU), Ms Margrethe Vestager gave the assurance during a visit to the Honourable Minister of Industry, Trade and Investments, Otunba Adeniyi Adebayo at the Old Federal Secretariat Complex in Abuja.
Vestager who is completing her 5-day working visit to Nigeria reiterated EU's commitment to intensify cooperation with Nigeria in the areas of agri-food, energy & infrastructure.
According to Ms Vestager, "I have trust in agriculture as it has the capacity to create access to trade channels that would otherwise be difficult."
She said the EU was open to holding high level talks with Nigerian authorities with a view to knowing specific priorities that benefit the citizenry.
"We are open to holding high level talks with Nigerian authorities to know specific areas of priority especially as it pertains the industrialization of the nation. In the coming days, we hope to intensify discussion that will lead to signing investment agreements with Nigeria which will facilitate industrialization.
"We have a lot of discussion going on in Europe that governments should produce in the areas that they think they have friends or next-door neighbours, and the consensus is that Nigeria qualifies because of its scope of investments and ease of doing business," Vestager said.
The EU Vice President tasked Nigerian authorities to strengthen its competition framework to encourage companies to offer consumer goods and services on the most favourable terms, in a manner that guarantees efficiency and innovation and reduced prices.
Speaking during the meeting the Minister of Industry, Trade and Investments, Adeniyi Adebayo said the focus of his ministry was industrialization programme with an overarching goal of driving job-intensive growth of the Nigerian economy through industrialisation.
Adebayo said the priority of the Trade and Investments Ministry is backward integration and domestication of key commodities like sugar, cassava starch, oil palm, automobile assembly and component manufacturing, cotton, textiles and garments.
"Like I told you yesterday and like you have heard everywhere you have been, President Muhammadu Buhari has the goal of taking a 100million Nigerians out of poverty over the next 10 years and we believe that that can be done through industrialisation.
"So, we have this backward integration policy and the whole idea is to domesticate our key commodities such as sugar, cassava starch, oil palm, automobile assembly and component manufacturing, cotton, textiles and garments. The whole idea is to be self-sufficient in these areas," Adebayo said.
He said opportunities exist within our Backward Integration Program (BIP) for investments in these commodities to increase production for local consumption and exports.
Expatiating on the nation's trade policy, Adebayo said Nigeria was ready to participate effectively in the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) - on the Rules of Origin, Technical Barriers to Trade Monitoring Capability, Establishment of Trade remedy Mechanism with a view to position Nigeria as a supply chain partner to leading global economies.
Adebayo said Nigeria's increasing mobile penetration rate, focused regulatory drive to boost broadband penetration and financial inclusion, are combining to create the perfect recipe for a thriving fintech sector and the adoption of digital solutions to solve everyday problems.
Speaking on the problems militating the growth of Small and Medium Enterprise (SMEs), the Minister of State for Industry, Trade and Investments, Ambassador Mariam Yelwaji Katagum listed multiple taxation, high interest rates, inadequate working capital, stiff competition from larger companies as some of the challenges confronting small businesses but said Federal Government was dealing with the challenges vide business reforms, superintended by the Presidential Enabling Business Environment Council (PEBEC) chaired by Vice President Yemi Osinbajo.
Katagum explained that areas of collaboration existed with multilateral and private sector financing of Special Economic Zones through public-private-partnerships and greenfield development.
"One area of collaboration which is important is support for women enterprises. I would like to know what the EU is doing in this regard. Others are credit facilitation for Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) financing programs and market access for exports
"We will also require support in facilitating discussions and matchmaking on investment opportunities to large investors as well as support in providing Best-Practice review and management/navigation of key business legislative reforms and Ease of Doing Business Reforms," Katagum said.

Original_Search_Title: Kenya - Tanzania to Build Grain Silos in Mombasa After Juba, Lubumbashi
Headline: Kenya - Tanzania to Build Grain Silos in Mombasa After Juba, Lubumbashi
Body_Text:
Tanzania is envisioning the construction of giant grain storage facilities and distribution centres in Mombasa to assist in selling surplus food crops from the country.
The proposed large Silos will facilitate the trading of surplus food crops between Kenya and Tanzania. The investment will improve bilateral trade between the two countries, which stood at US$905.5 million from January to November 2021.
Tanzania is ambitious about the grain storage facilities as the country is already operating established two-grain storage spaces in the Democratic Republic of Congo and South Sudan.
Through the Mombasa grain centre, Tanzania will be supplying agricultural products directly to Kenya. Tanzanian President Samia Suluhu Hassan and Kenyan President Uhuru Kenyatta agreed to harmonize trade barriers between the two countries resulting in a better trading environment.
The grain storage facility will be used by both the government and the private sector.
The Minister of Agriculture, Hussein Bashe, revealed on his Twitter page the intention of Tanzania setting up a grain store in Kenya's coastal city island of Mombasa. Bashe added that the ministry would not directly involve itself in selling the grain exports but would act as an initiator.
"We have opened grain centres in Juba, South Sudan, and Lubumbashi in the DRC and already delivered 800 tonnes of grains to the two centres, in efforts to enable Tanzanian farmers and traders to get good markets for their products," Bashe posted. "We believe access to markets motivates farmers to increase production."
The Tanzania Cereals and Other produce Board (CPB) reported that the grain stores in Juba and Lubumbashi were already experiencing a vibrant business environment. They also expected the Mombasa facility to outperform the two.
According to the 2019 Statistics, Tanzania exported more than 97,000 tonnes of maize. This factor opened the country to the opportunity of launching its grain surplus scheme with the Southern Agricultural Growth Corridor of Tanzania (SAGCOT).
SAGCOT was established in 2010 to create a transformed and economically viable agricultural sector in Tanzania that protects food security, enhances environmental sustainability, and improves livelihoods. The Agricultural growth corridor uses 350,000 hectares in the fertile southern islands of Tanzania to grow maize, wheat, paddy, sorghum, cassava, millet, beans, bananas and sweet potatoes.
World Bank, a significant funder of SAGCOT, said that its objective is to increase the adoption of new advanced technologies and improve the marketing strategies of smallholder farmers. This will, in turn, expand and create partnerships between the agribusiness sector in Tanzania and the smallholder farmers.
Recently, Tanzania has been posting food surplus in the agricultural harvest. In the 2021/2022 financial year, the East African country reported a food surplus of over 3.6 million metric tonnes.
The Mombasa grain store is not the first time Tanzania has planned on investment in Kenya. Bakhresa Group Limited, a food processing entity, had in the past expressed interest in setting up a maize mill facility in Mombasa, but the project never materialized.
With the eased trade barriers between the two countries, the trade will increase exponentially due to the free movement of factors of production.
The mining sector in Tanzania is also doing well in the export market.
Mining in Tanzania
Mining and extraction activities contributed about 10.2 per cent of Tanzania's gross domestic product in the first quarter of 2021. The country earned approximately US$2.3 billion through mineral exports in 2019.
The mining sector includes small- and large-scale mining of metals (gold, nickel, iron ore, cobalt, copper and silver), industrial minerals (diamonds, garnet, limestone, soda ash, salt, ruby, phosphate, gravel, gypsum, sand, dimension stones, tanzanite and graphite), and fuel minerals (coal and uranium).
Gold has the highest contribution to the value of mineral exports. Tanzania is the 4th largest gold producer in Africa.
The mining industry in the East African country remains a goldmine to investors and offers significant potential in the diversification of nickel, coal and uranium mining. The government also offers investment incentives and supply chain opportunities in the mining sector.
